treatment.title: Haemodialysis: cleaning the blood when the kidneys no longer can
treatment.p1: When the kidneys can no longer filter waste and excess water, haemodialysis takes over. Blood is carried to the dialysis machine through a fistula (a vascular access created in the arm), filtered in a dialyser (an artificial filter), then returned to the body.
treatment.p2: Each session lasts about 4 hours. Most patients come 3 times a week, as prescribed by the nephrologist. During the session, you are settled comfortably on a 3-position bed, under continuous monitoring by our nurses and a doctor present on site.

seance.title: What a session at ESSAADA includes
seance.subtitle: Concretely, here is what you receive at every visit — not as an option, not as an extra.
seance.note: Sessions are contracted with CNAS and CASNOS — no out-of-pocket payment if you are affiliated. Our administrative team prepares your file with you.

seanceIncludes: Weigh-in, blood pressure check and pre-session assessment
A 4-hour session on a new dialysis machine
Continuous monitoring by dedicated nursing staff
A nephrologist present on site throughout every session
Computerised traceability of your parameters at each session
A companion allowed in the dedicated waiting room
Full CNAS / CASNOS coverage — no out-of-pocket payment
A report sent to your treating nephrologist
The front-desk WhatsApp for your questions between sessions

hero.title: Nephrology consultation
hero.subtitle: For the diagnosis, follow-up and prevention of kidney disease at every stage.

intro.p1: A consultation with a nephrologist is recommended when your GP identifies kidney abnormalities, or in the case of an at-risk condition (diabetes, high blood pressure, family history).
intro.p2: The nephrologist listens to you, reviews your records, may prescribe further tests, then works with you to build a clear follow-up plan. The aim: to slow the progression of the disease, anticipate any future need for dialysis, and preserve your quality of life.

bullets: Preparation for dialysis when the time comes

pullQuote.quoteLine1: “10% of kidney failures can be prevented,
pullQuote.quoteLine2: and 30% can be delayed by several years.”
pullQuote.caption: This is why we are developing nephrology consultations as close as possible to patients: to make an early diagnosis, slow the progression of the disease, and keep you in good health for as long as possible before renal replacement therapy becomes necessary.

etp.title: Learning to live with the disease, not against it
etp.subtitle: Alongside treatment, therapeutic patient education strengthens your independence: you become an active participant in your own care, not a bystander.

timeline.title: Your first session, minute by minute
timeline.subtitle: Because the unknown is more unsettling than the known, here is exactly what happens — in order, with no surprises.
timeline.note: You are never alone, at any point. That is the rule here.

arrivee.title: You arrive at the clinic
arrivee.text: Wheelchair access. Take your time — our time slots are designed so that you are never rushed.

accueil.title: Welcome and observations
accueil.text: Weight, blood pressure, temperature. The nurse notes how you are feeling at that moment. A relative may stay with you at this stage.

consultation.title: Brief consultation with the nephrologist
consultation.text: The doctor reviews your file, adjusts your session settings if needed and answers your questions. You decide together.

installation.title: Settling in and needle insertion
installation.text: Reclining chair, blanket, pillow. Insertion of the fistula needles (or connection of the catheter) — 2 to 3 minutes. The nurse explains each step to you.

debut.title: Start of the session
debut.text: The machine starts up. You can read, sleep, look at your phone or listen to music.

surveillance.title: Continuous monitoring
surveillance.text: The machine measures your blood pressure automatically. A nurse checks on you every 15 minutes. A nephrologist is on the unit. Coffee is served midway through the session.

fin.title: End of the session
fin.text: Needle removal, compression, dressing. Discharge weight, a final blood pressure reading, a final word with the nurse. Your next appointment is given to you.

depart.title: You head home
depart.text: A little rest if needed in the waiting room. Your relative can accompany you home.

cta.title: Questions before your first visit?
cta.subtitle: Opening hours, companions, documents to bring, meals — our team explains everything in advance.
cta.waMessage: Hello, I have a practical question to prepare for my visit to ESSAADA Clinic.

items.1: No session after f'tour, no night-time slot
items.2: Rescheduling in consultation with your nephrologist

items.0: A companion may be present during the first sessions

items.2: A doctor on site at all times

sport.title: Sport and physical activity
sport.lead: Staying active is good for the body, the spirits and the treatment itself — it is strongly encouraged.

body.0: Walking, swimming, cycling, gardening, stretching: any regular physical activity is beneficial during the dialysis journey. It helps to maintain muscle tone, protects the heart, and improves sleep and mood on non-session days. There is no reason to put your life on hold.
body.1: For a more demanding return to sport — running, weight training, team sports, high-altitude hiking — a word with the nephrologist helps to adjust things according to your fistula, your blood pressure and your general fitness. The aim is never to forbid, but to adapt.

sport.tip: Protect your fistula arm from knocks and heavy loads — the rest of your body remains free.

voyages.title: Travel and journeys
voyages.lead: Going away on holiday, visiting family in France or back home, performing the Umrah: all of this remains possible.

body.0: A trip is prepared with the team a few weeks in advance. We help to find a host centre at your destination — in Algeria as well as abroad — and to forward your medical file so that your sessions continue without interruption. Haemodialysis centres exist everywhere: Algiers, Oran, Marseille, Lyon, Paris, Mecca, Istanbul.
body.1: Remember to plan ahead for the administrative formalities (agreement of cover, travel insurance, prescriptions translated if needed). For patients from the diaspora returning to spend the summer in Sidi Bel Abbès, the arrangements are made in both directions with the same care.

voyages.tip: Ideally let us know 4 to 6 weeks before departure so we can calmly arrange the sessions at your destination.

travail.title: Working life
travail.lead: Continuing to work is often possible, and it is a real support — financial, social and personal.

body.0: Dialysis sessions set the rhythm of the week, but many patients keep up a working life. Early-morning or end-of-day slots free up much of the working day. As far as possible, we try to fit your sessions around your schedule constraints.
body.1: In Algeria, recognition as a disabled worker can be applied for through the social services: it sometimes opens the way to adjustments (working hours, role) or to a pension depending on the situation. Your treating doctor and the social worker can guide you through the process; there is no guarantee of success, but it is worth raising.

travail.tip: Tell us about your working hours from the very first appointment — we build the schedule together.

intimite.title: Intimacy and couple life
intimite.lead: Kidney disease also affects intimate life — it is common, and it can be discussed without embarrassment during consultations.

body.0: Reduced desire, fatigue, erectile difficulties in men, dryness or cycle irregularities in women: these changes affect more than one patient in two. They combine the psychological burden of the illness, the treatments, and very real hormonal changes. It is neither all in the mind nor inevitable.
body.1: The nephrologist is there to talk about it, without judgement and in complete confidence — just like blood pressure or sleep. Solutions exist, sometimes simple ones: adjusting treatments, the opinion of a specialist colleague, psychological support for the couple. Talking about it already lifts part of the burden.

intimite.tip: No question is out of place during a consultation. Intimacy is part of overall health.

grossesse.title: Pregnancy and parenthood
grossesse.lead: Fertility is reduced in kidney failure, but a pregnancy remains possible — it must be carefully prepared.

body.0: For a woman on dialysis, carrying a pregnancy involves real risks for both mother and child: foetal growth restriction, premature birth, maternal complications. It is not forbidden, but it requires close monitoring between nephrologist, gynaecologist and maternity unit, with dialysis sessions often intensified during the nine months.
body.1: When a plan to have a child is in mind, reliable contraception is most often recommended while it is discussed calmly, and where possible postponing pregnancy until after a kidney transplant: transplantation markedly improves fertility and lowers the risks. Raise it early with your nephrologist — it is an important conversation, to be approached as such.

grossesse.tip: A plan for a baby, contraception to review: our support includes these questions.

alimentation.title: Diet
alimentation.lead: Eating well on dialysis is above all about adapting well — there is no single diet that suits everyone.

body.0: Needs evolve throughout the journey: before dialysis, the aim is often to slow the worsening of kidney disease. Once sessions have begun, the priorities change — correcting excess water, potassium or phosphorus, but also preventing malnutrition, because eating enough remains essential. Diabetes, high blood pressure or other associated conditions further alter the balance.
body.1: The nephrologist links your blood test results and adjusts the recommendations to your real situation — your habits, your cooking, what you enjoy — rather than to a theoretical list of forbidden foods. The aim is a balance that is sustainable over time, not an impossible diet to follow.

alimentation.tip: Talk about your diet during a consultation whenever you feel the need — it is designed for you, not against you.

hero.title: Your questions, our answers
hero.subtitle: The essential information to understand haemodialysis and your care journey.

cta.title: Can't find the answer to your question here?
cta.subtitle: Our team answers your questions by WhatsApp, telephone or during a consultation.
cta.waMessage: Hello, I have a question about dialysis that isn't in the FAQ.

cost.question: How much does a session cost? Do I have to pay anything?
cost.answer: Nothing if you are affiliated with CNAS or CASNOS. The clinic is approved (conventionnée): your haemodialysis sessions are fully covered through direct billing (tiers-payant) — you pay nothing upfront. Our administrative team prepares your file with you (Chifa card + CNAS or CASNOS certificate). If anything is holding it up, we tell you straight away and guide you through the process.

companion.question: Can my companion stay with me?
companion.answer: Yes. We are happy to welcome a companion, especially for the first sessions. A dedicated waiting room is available to them (coffee and tea served).

sessionDuration.question: How long does a dialysis session last?
sessionDuration.answer: A haemodialysis session lasts about 4 hours. The exact duration is determined by your nephrologist according to your needs. You can read, watch television or rest during the session.

frequency.question: How often will I come in?
frequency.answer: Most patients come 3 times a week. Your doctor adjusts the frequency to your clinical situation.

painGeneral.question: Does dialysis hurt?
painGeneral.answer: Needling the fistula (the vascular access created in the arm) may cause discomfort comparable to a blood test. Our nurses are trained to keep it to a minimum. Once the session has started, it is painless.

emergency.question: What happens if I feel faint during the session?
emergency.answer: A nephrologist is present on site for the entire duration of the sessions — this is not a telephone on-call arrangement, it is an actual physical presence. Our nursing team is trained in emergency protocols and intervenes immediately in the event of a drop in blood pressure or faintness.

work.question: Can I keep working while on dialysis?
work.answer: Yes, in most cases. We arrange your time slots (morning, daytime or evening) so they fit around your schedule. Many of our patients continue their working or family life.

eating.question: Can I eat during dialysis?
eating.answer: Yes. We recommend a light meal 2 hours before coming in, and coffee is served during the session.

firstAppointment.question: How do I book a first appointment?
firstAppointment.answer: Call us or send a WhatsApp message. Our team offers you an appointment with a nephrologist within 24 to 48 hours (working days). We can also arrange a no-obligation introductory visit to the centre.

transfer.question: I already dialyse elsewhere — how do I transfer my care?
transfer.answer: Contact us with your most recent laboratory results (full blood count + biochemistry), your serology (HBV, HCV, HIV) and your dialysis prescription. Our nephrologist gets in touch with your treating doctor to ensure continuity of care, without any interruption.

cnasCasnos.question: Are you approved by CNAS and CASNOS?
cnasCasnos.answer: Yes. The clinic is approved with CNAS (Caisse Nationale des Assurances Sociales des travailleurs salariés) and CASNOS (Caisse Nationale de Sécurité Sociale des Non-Salariés). Bring your Chifa card and your medical file to your first appointment.

visiting.question: I'm on holiday in the area — can I have dialysis with you?
visiting.answer: Yes. We welcome visiting patients (both residents in Algeria and members of the diaspora). Your slot is confirmed within 72 working hours. Contact us as soon as possible with your most recent laboratory results (full blood count + biochemistry), your serology (HBV, HCV, HIV) and your dialysis prescription. See the dedicated "Visiting patients" page.

ramadan.question: Are the hours adapted during Ramadan?
ramadan.answer: Yes. During Ramadan, we open a single series of slots with a first session at 4 a.m. No session after f'tour, no night-time slot. Rescheduling is agreed together with your nephrologist. See the "Ramadan & dialysis" page.

accessibility.question: Is the clinic accessible to people with reduced mobility?
accessibility.answer: Yes. The clinic has access ramps for wheelchairs, reserved parking spaces near the entrance, and direct access for ambulances. Our staff can assist you if needed.

firstSessionFear.question: My very first session here, and I'm really frightened. What actually happens?
firstSessionFear.answer: It is completely normal to feel apprehensive; almost all our patients feel this way the first time. Here is what to expect: on arrival, the nurse welcomes you, checks your weight and blood pressure, then settles you comfortably into a reclining chair with a blanket. Inserting the needles takes 2 to 3 minutes, and we explain each step. You are then connected to the machine for about 4 hours. Throughout the session, a nurse comes by every 15 minutes, and a nephrologist is present in the unit. You can read, sleep or look at your phone. Coffee is served to you. At the end of the session, the needles are removed, we check that all is well, and you leave. You are never alone.

needlesPain.question: Do the needles hurt?
needlesPain.answer: We prefer to be honest: the needling is unpleasant, but it lasts barely 10 seconds. It is not a sharp pain, more a strong sensation that quickly passes. If you have a fistula (AVF), an anaesthetic cream can be applied an hour before the session to make the needling almost painless. If you have a central catheter, there is no needling at all, just a connection. Most of our patients tell us that after 3 or 4 sessions the apprehension fades and the procedure becomes routine. Our nurses are trained in gentle needling — if a needle hurts you, say so immediately and we adjust.

emergencyVacation.question: I'm on holiday in Algeria but I didn't give notice. Can you take me on urgently?
emergencyVacation.answer: Yes, this is possible subject to availability, and we do everything we can to leave no one without a session. Contact us immediately by WhatsApp on the centre's number, even in the evening or at the weekend. If possible, have ready: your most recent dialysis report (dry weight, session time, treatment), your recent serology (hepatitis B/C, HIV), your current prescription, and a piece of identification. If we are full at the time you want, we offer you another slot during the week or refer you to a partner centre. Never skip a session — call us and we will find a solution.

serologyAge.question: My serology is 4 months old — is it still valid?
serologyAge.answer: The standard is that serology (hepatitis B, C and HIV) must be less than 3 months old to be dialysed at a centre. At 4 months it is no longer accepted; this is a strict health rule to protect every patient in the unit. Don't worry: we work with a partner laboratory in Sidi Bel Abbès that can repeat the tests quickly. The blood draw takes 10 minutes and the results come back within 24 to 48 hours. We advise you to have the blood test as soon as you arrive in Algeria, even before your first scheduled session.

companionDuringSession.question: Can a loved one stay with me during the 4-hour session?
companionDuringSession.answer: We understand how reassuring the presence of a loved one is, especially the first few times. They cannot stay continuously in the dialysis room for reasons of hygiene and the privacy of other patients, but they have a dedicated waiting room with comfortable seating and a water point. They can come to see you briefly during the session, generally 2 to 3 times, subject to the nurse's agreement. You keep your phone within reach, so you can message them at any time. For your very first session, we are more flexible and allow a slightly longer visit while you are being settled in.

dataProtection.question: Is my medical data protected?
dataProtection.answer: Yes. We strictly apply Algerian Law 18-07 on the protection of personal data. Your medical file is kept securely, accessible only to the care team looking after you: nephrologist, nurses and authorised administrative staff. No data is passed on to a third party (employer, private insurer, extended family) without your written consent. At any time you can request a copy of your file, correct any erroneous information, or ask for the deletion of data that is not medically necessary. For diaspora patients, we send your stay report to your nephrologist in France by secure email, solely at your request.

meta.description: 29 key terms in nephrology and dialysis explained simply: vascular access, creatinine, GFR, fistula, haemodialysis, transplant, urea. By Clinique ESSAADA.
meta.ogDescription: The vocabulary of dialysis and kidney failure, defined simply. 29 entries, from the most technical to the most everyday.

hero.title: Glossary — the words of dialysis, explained simply
hero.subtitle: 29 terms you will come across in consultation, on your dialysis record, or in conversations with the team. No jargon: accessible, verifiable definitions, with no scaremongering.

ctaBlock.title: Still puzzled by a term?
ctaBlock.body: This glossary does not replace a conversation with your nephrologist. During consultation, ask all your questions — none is out of place, and none is too simple.

term.subtitle: A clear, jargon-free definition, verified by the medical team at Clinique ESSAADA.
term.furtherTitle: A question about your care pathway ?
term.furtherSubtitle: Our nephrology team answers your questions during consultation.

acces-vasculaire.question: What is vascular access?
acces-vasculaire.definition: A surgical or medical entry route that connects the bloodstream to the dialysis machine. In haemodialysis, the two main forms are the arteriovenous fistula (a long-lasting solution) and the catheter (used in emergencies or while awaiting a fistula). The quality of vascular access directly determines the effectiveness of sessions and the patient's long-term safety.

ase.question: What is an ESA in dialysis?
ase.definition: Short for "erythropoiesis-stimulating agent". These are injectable medicines that mimic the action of erythropoietin, the hormone normally produced by the kidneys. They are prescribed to patients with kidney failure whose kidneys no longer make enough of this hormone, in order to correct renal anaemia and reduce the tiredness caused by a lack of red blood cells.

catheter.question: What is a dialysis catheter?
catheter.definition: A soft, thin plastic tube inserted into a blood vessel (often in the neck or groin) to gain access to the circulation. In haemodialysis it serves as temporary vascular access, particularly in emergencies or while a fistula is maturing. It can also be placed in the abdomen for peritoneal dialysis. Handling it requires strict hygiene.

centre-hemodialyse.question: What is a haemodialysis centre?
centre-hemodialyse.definition: A care facility dedicated to patients with severe kidney failure who require comprehensive management. A nephrologist is present at all times during sessions, supported by a nursing team. This setting suits patients whose medical condition calls for close monitoring, as opposed to self-care dialysis or home dialysis.

creatinine.question: What is creatinine?
creatinine.definition: A waste product continuously produced by the normal functioning of the muscles, from creatine. It is eliminated by the kidneys in the urine. Measuring it in the blood and calculating its clearance make it possible to assess kidney function: the more creatinine builds up in the blood, the less effectively the kidneys are filtering. It is one of the reference markers in nephrology follow-up.

diabete.question: What is diabetes?
diabete.definition: A chronic disease marked by an excessively high level of glucose (sugar) in the blood, linked to a defect in the production or action of insulin. Poorly controlled over the long term, it damages the small blood vessels, particularly those of the kidneys. About one in five diabetic patients develops kidney failure; it is one of the leading causes of dialysis worldwide.

dialyse.question: What is dialysis?
dialyse.definition: A treatment that partially replaces the work of failing kidneys by clearing the blood of its waste products and excess water. Two techniques exist: haemodialysis, which filters the blood through an artificial kidney, and peritoneal dialysis, which uses the natural membrane of the abdomen. The choice depends on the patient's medical condition, lifestyle and preferences.

dialyse-a-domicile.question: What is home dialysis?
dialyse-a-domicile.definition: A treatment option in which the patient carries out their own dialysis at home, after supervised training. It offers great independence, better respect for personal routines, and regular but less frequent medical follow-up. It mainly concerns peritoneal dialysis, and more rarely home haemodialysis. It requires a suitable environment and a relative available to provide support.

dialyse-peritoneale.question: What is peritoneal dialysis?
dialyse-peritoneale.definition: A technique that uses the peritoneum, the thin membrane lining the abdominal cavity, as a natural filter. A dialysis solution is introduced into the abdomen through a catheter, left in place for a few hours, then drained and replaced. The treatment works continuously, 24 hours a day, more closely imitating the natural kidney. It is performed almost exclusively at home, after the patient has been trained.

dialyseur.question: What is a dialyser?
dialyseur.definition: The central filter of the haemodialysis machine, sometimes called an "artificial kidney". It is made up of thousands of semi-permeable hollow fibres through which the blood flows. Waste products and excess water pass from the blood into the dialysis solution, while the blood cells and proteins remain in the circulation. It is the dialyser that actually carries out the purification.

dpa.question: What is APD?
dpa.definition: Short for "automated peritoneal dialysis". The patient connects to a small device, the cycler, which automatically carries out the exchanges of dialysis solution in the abdomen during the night, over roughly 8 to 10 hours. The daytime is thus free of handling. This technique suits people who are active or find the manual exchanges of CAPD inconvenient.

dpca.question: What is CAPD?
dpca.definition: Short for "continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis". The patient carries out four to five exchanges of dialysis solution in the abdomen by hand during the day, at regular intervals. Each exchange lasts about 30 minutes. Between two exchanges, they can lead a normal life. This method requires no electrical device and can be carried out anywhere, provided hygiene is respected.

epo.question: What is EPO?
epo.definition: A common abbreviation for "erythropoietin", a hormone produced by healthy kidneys that stimulates the bone marrow to make red blood cells. In patients with kidney failure, EPO production falls, which causes anaemia. Synthetic forms are then prescribed by injection to restore a satisfactory haemoglobin level and reduce tiredness.

erythropoietine.question: What is erythropoietin?
erythropoietine.definition: A hormone naturally produced by healthy kidneys. It acts on the bone marrow, stimulating it to produce red blood cells, which carry oxygen. When the kidneys are diseased, this production decreases and renal anaemia sets in, causing tiredness and breathlessness. Synthetic versions (EPO, ESA) help to make up for this deficiency.

fistule-arterio-veineuse.question: What is an arteriovenous fistula?
fistule-arterio-veineuse.definition: The reference vascular access for haemodialysis, created surgically, most often in the forearm. The surgeon connects an artery to a nearby vein: the vein gradually thickens under the arterial pressure and becomes easy to needle. It provides the high blood flow required for sessions. Its maturation takes several weeks before the first use.

greffe-de-rein.question: What is a kidney transplant?
greffe-de-rein.definition: A surgical procedure that replaces the function of diseased kidneys with a healthy kidney from a donor. The donor may be living, often a compatible relative, or deceased. When it is possible, a transplant offers a quality of life close to normal and frees the patient from dialysis sessions. It requires lifelong immunosuppressive treatment.

hemodialyse.question: What is haemodialysis?
hemodialyse.definition: A dialysis technique in which the patient's blood circulates outside the body through an artificial kidney, the dialyser. Waste products and excess water pass into the dialysis solution through a semi-permeable membrane, then the cleansed blood returns to the patient. Sessions usually take place three times a week and last four to six hours.

hemoglobine.question: What is haemoglobin?
hemoglobine.definition: A protein contained in the red blood cells, responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the tissues. It owes its red colour to the iron it contains. When it falls below a certain threshold, this is called anaemia, which shows up as tiredness, breathlessness and pallor. Anaemia is common in dialysis patients and is corrected with treatment.

insuffisance-renale-aigue.question: What is acute kidney injury?
insuffisance-renale-aigue.definition: A sudden loss, within a few hours or days, of the kidneys' ability to filter the blood. It is often temporary and reversible if managed quickly. Common causes are a drop in blood supply to the kidneys, an obstruction of the urinary tract, or damage following an operation, an accident or a serious infection. Treatment is often given in intensive care.

insuffisance-renale-chronique-terminale.question: What is end-stage kidney failure?
insuffisance-renale-chronique-terminale.definition: The most advanced stage of chronic kidney disease, where kidney function is below roughly 10% of normal (assessed by the GFR, or glomerular filtration rate). At this stage, the kidneys can no longer cleanse the blood on their own. Replacement therapy then becomes necessary: dialysis or a kidney transplant.

maladie-renale-chronique.question: What is chronic kidney disease?
maladie-renale-chronique.definition: A slow and progressive decline in kidney function, developing over several years. It is often silent at first and discovered through blood or urine tests. Without management, it can progress to end-stage kidney failure, then requiring dialysis or a transplant. Its main causes are diabetes, high blood pressure and certain inherited diseases.

medicaments-immunosuppresseurs.question: What is an immunosuppressant medicine?
medicaments-immunosuppresseurs.definition: Medicines that dampen the activity of the immune system. They are prescribed for life after a kidney transplant to prevent the body from recognising the graft as foreign and rejecting it. Their balance is delicate: too little and rejection threatens, too much and the patient becomes vulnerable to infections. They require close and regular medical follow-up.

pression-arterielle.question: What is blood pressure?
pression-arterielle.definition: The pressure exerted by the blood on the walls of the arteries with each heartbeat. When it remains too high over time, this is called hypertension, which strains the heart, the blood vessels and the kidneys. It increases the risk of heart attack and stroke, and is among the main causes of chronic kidney disease. It is treated through lifestyle measures and medication.

solution-de-dialyse.question: What is dialysis solution?
solution-de-dialyse.definition: A sterile fluid made up of ultra-purified water, glucose and electrolytes (sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, chlorides). In the dialyser it flows on the opposite side of the membrane from the blood: waste products and excess water cross over through the difference in concentration, while the useful elements are preserved. Its composition is adjusted to each patient.

temps-de-stase.question: What is dwell time?
temps-de-stase.definition: In peritoneal dialysis, the period during which the fresh dialysis solution remains in the abdomen to carry out the purification. Its length varies according to the technique, from a few hours during the day to a full night. At the end of this time, the dialysate laden with waste is drained and replaced with fresh solution, either by hand by the patient or automatically by a cycler.

ultrafiltration.question: What is ultrafiltration?
ultrafiltration.definition: The mechanism by which the excess water built up in the blood is removed during dialysis. The amount to be removed is calculated before each session from the patient's weight and their target "dry weight". Well-judged ultrafiltration relieves breathlessness and oedema; if too fast, it can lead to cramps or a drop in blood pressure. The team adjusts the settings accordingly.

unite-auto-dialyse.question: What is a self-care dialysis unit (SDU)?
unite-auto-dialyse.definition: A facility where patients, trained and fully or partly independent, carry out their own haemodialysis session under the light supervision of a nurse. Sessions take place in small groups, often around five patients to one carer, in a friendly setting. This arrangement suits medically stable people who wish to retain their independence and social life.

unite-dialyse-medicalisee.question: What is a medicalised dialysis unit?
unite-dialyse-medicalisee.definition: A facility intermediate between the full haemodialysis centre and self-care dialysis. It takes in patients whose sessions usually run without incident, but who cannot manage their own care. A nursing team is present at all times, and a nephrologist attends on occasion, without continuous medical supervision during the session itself.

uree.question: What is urea?
uree.definition: A waste product arising from the normal breakdown of proteins by the liver, carried by the blood and eliminated by the kidneys in the urine. In patients with kidney failure, it builds up in the blood and its level serves as an indicator of the remaining kidney function. Too high a level can cause tiredness, loss of appetite and nausea; one aim of dialysis is to bring it back down.

meta.title: Dry weight & hydration measurement · Bioimpedance
meta.description: Multi-frequency bioimpedance (BIS): objective measurement of dry weight, hydration and body composition. A precision tool to personalise every haemodialysis session at Clinique ESSAADA, Sidi Bel Abbès.
meta.ogTitle: Precise dry weight & hydration measurement — Clinique ESSAADA
meta.ogDescription: Multi-frequency bioimpedance: a precision tool to adjust your dry weight and track your body composition, integrated into the dialysis protocol.

hero.title: Measuring your dry weight and hydration precisely
hero.subtitle: Multi-frequency bioimpedance — the same measurement technology used in European nephrology centres, integrated into your dialysis follow-up.
hero.photoAlt: Bodystat Multiscan 5000 — medical body composition analyser using multi-frequency bioimpedance, touchscreen showing the New Test / Recall Data / Options / Information menu

why.title: Going beyond the ordinary bathroom scales
why.p1: In haemodialysis, weight is only part of the story. What really matters is the distribution between water, lean mass and fat mass. Ordinary scales cannot tell these apart.
why.p2: Multi-frequency bioimpedance (BIS) sends an imperceptible electrical current at several frequencies through your body. Depending on the frequency, this current crosses cell membranes to a greater or lesser extent — which makes it possible to separate total body water, intracellular water, extracellular water, lean mass and fat mass.
why.p3: It is a tool that complements the nephrologist's clinical assessment. It does not replace their judgement — it enriches it with objective data, tracked over time.

device.title: The device used
device.note: Multi-frequency bioimpedance is used routinely in certain European nephrology centres. It remains higher-end equipment, still uncommon in Algeria.

pillars.title: Three concrete contributions to your follow-up
pillars.subtitle: What this tool helps your nephrologist do better — and what it does not claim to do.

drySetting.title: Adjusting the dry weight
drySetting.text: The objective measurement of body water (total, intracellular, extracellular) helps your nephrologist fine-tune your dry weight, beyond clinical assessment alone. The aim: to limit intradialytic hypotension and fluid overload between sessions.

bodyComposition.title: Tracking body composition
bodyComposition.text: Lean mass, skeletal muscle mass, fat mass — tracked over time to detect muscle wasting (sarcopenia) or malnutrition, both common in end-stage kidney failure.

phaseAngle.title: Reading the phase angle
phaseAngle.text: The phase angle reflects the integrity of your cells. A marker recognised in the nephrology literature, it enriches the nutritional assessment and the general condition of the dialysis patient.

steps.title: A measurement built into your visit, in four steps
steps.subtitle: No separate appointment: the measurement is taken before or after a session, within the unit.

setup.title: Setting up
setup.text: You lie down comfortably, fully clothed. No fasting, no injection required.

electrodes.title: Placing the electrodes
electrodes.text: Four adhesive electrodes (two on the hand, two on the foot), like an ECG. Painless.

measurement.title: Measurement
measurement.text: The device sends an imperceptible current at several frequencies (5 to 1000 kHz). Duration: about two minutes.

reading.title: Reading & decision
reading.text: The nephrologist interprets the results with you and adjusts your dialysis prescription if needed.

report.title: What the report handed to your nephrologist looks like
report.subtitle: Each indicator is placed on a visual scale (red / orange / green) showing where you stand relative to the reference ranges. The nephrologist discusses each zone with you.
report.alt1: Bodystat report — results of the body water analysis (TBW, ICW, ECW, OHY) and composition (weight, fat mass, lean mass, muscle mass), with red / orange / green coloured bars placing each value relative to the reference ranges.
report.alt2: Bodystat report — cellular health (phase angle, membrane capacitance), basal metabolic rate, multi-frequency impedance measurements and the report's summary conclusion.
report.disclaimer: Sample report — anonymised data. Your report is always discussed by your nephrologist, never handed over raw without interpretation.

when.title: Three moments where this measurement makes the most sense

firstSession.title: At your first session
firstSession.text: To establish a clear starting point: initial dry weight, hydration status, baseline body composition.

regularFollowUp.title: During regular follow-up
regularFollowUp.text: Periodic measurements to readjust your dry weight and detect malnutrition or muscle wasting early.

visitingPatients.title: Visiting patients
visitingPatients.text: For an extended stay (two weeks or more), a measurement may be offered to personalise your care — at no extra cost for CNAS / CASNOS members.

honesty.title: What this tool does — and does not do
honesty.does1: Helps adjust the dry weight, alongside clinical judgement
honesty.does4: Provides the phase angle, a marker of cellular integrity
honesty.doesNot1: It does not determine your dry weight on its own — it helps your doctor do so
honesty.doesNot2: It does not diagnose malnutrition on its own
honesty.doesNot3: It does not replace blood tests or the clinical examination
honesty.doesNot4: It measures neither cancer nor heart disease — it is not a screening test
honesty.note: Bioimpedance is a tool to support medical decision-making. Interpretation is the nephrologist's responsibility, who integrates it into your clinical file.

partOf.title: Is this measurement part of my follow-up?
partOf.text: If you are a patient at Clinique ESSAADA or are preparing for a dialysis stay, speak to your nephrologist at your next consultation. We will explain when and how to use it in your case.

hero.title: The quality of your dialysis begins with the water and the equipment
hero.subtitle: A haemodialysis session brings your blood into contact with around 120 litres of treated water. At that volume, every detail matters. Here is what we put in place, with no simplification.

brand.title: Make and model

maintenance.title: Maintenance
maintenance.text: Regular preventive technical inspection by an approved provider, with traceability of interventions station by station. A biomedical technician is present during sessions.

traceability.title: Traceability
traceability.text: Parameters of every session recorded — pressure, flow rates, conductivity, effective dialysis duration.

water.title: Five stages, one ultrapure water
water.subtitle: Mains water passes through a treatment chain before entering the machine. A looped, sterilisable distribution network with no dead legs.

step2.title: Water softener
step2.text: Removes the calcium and magnesium that would scale the membranes.

step3.title: Activated carbon filter
step3.text: Captures chlorine and its by-products, which are toxic to the blood.

step4.title: 0.2 μm microfiltration
step4.text: A physical barrier against residual bacteria.

step5.title: Double-pass reverse osmosis
step5.text: The final stage, producing ultrapure water that meets dialysis standards.

controls.label: Regular checks:

depuisLaFrance.anticipate: Allow 6 to 8 weeks between starting your formalities and your departure. The CPAM does not process applications at short notice, and no session outside the EU is reimbursed without prior agreement obtained before departure.

steps.title: The 3 CPAM steps
steps.subtitle: A clear timeline, familiar points of contact. We support you through each one.

step1.title: CPAM prior agreement
step1.text: Log in to your ameli account or make an appointment with your fund. Request an administrative agreement to cover dialysis outside the EU. Without this agreement obtained before departure, no reimbursement will be possible.

step2.title: SE 352-05 I and II forms
step2.text: Provided for under the bilateral France–Algeria convention, to be completed with your treating nephrologist (medical section) and your CPAM (administrative section). Form I certifies your entitlements, form II formalises the request for scheduled care.

step3.title: Send us your file
step3.text: By email or WhatsApp, at the latest 3 weeks before your arrival. Our medical team validates your file within 48 working hours and confirms your slots.

docs.title: What we need from your treating nephrologist
docs.doc1: Up-to-date dialysis prescription (frequency, duration, dialyser, anticoagulation)
docs.doc2: HBV, HCV, HIV serologies dated less than 3 months ago
docs.doc4: Dialysis logbook for the last 3 months
docs.doc5: Latest Kt/V and reference dry weight
docs.doc6: Copy of your CPAM agreement and of the SE 352-05 forms

taxi.text: Roughly 4,000 — 5,000 DZD. To be negotiated as a flat fare before departure.

shuttle.title: Shuttle on request
shuttle.text: Can be arranged if you let us know at least 48h in advance. Fare provided with the quote.

faq.title: The practical questions you ask yourself before coming

q1.question: Can I dialyse with you if I do not yet have the CPAM agreement?
q1.answer: Yes, you can be treated, but no reimbursement will be possible on your return without the prior agreement obtained before departure. We strongly recommend waiting for the CPAM's response before finalising your trip.

q2.question: Will my top-up insurer cover the amount above €86.95?
q2.answer: That depends on your policy. Many top-up insurers complete the CPAM allowance for care abroad. Contact them before departure with your quote — we provide a personalised one after reviewing your file.

q3.question: Do the serologies and documents need to be translated?
q3.answer: No. Documents in French are accepted without translation. Reports in English are also accepted.

q4.question: Are your slots flexible if my flight is delayed?
q4.answer: Yes. Let us know as soon as possible via WhatsApp: we move your session to the nearest available slot, with no additional charge.

q5.question: How do I settle the invoice on site?
q5.answer: An original paid invoice is always handed to you, in the format required by the CPAM (itemised, stamped, in French). The accepted methods of payment are stated in your personalised quote.

q6.question: Can I come in an emergency without notice?
q6.answer: We give priority to files prepared in advance, for your safety and for the running of the unit. In a life-threatening emergency during your stay, call 14 (SAMU Algeria). We do everything we can to welcome you as soon as your condition is stable.

q7.question: Can I dialyse at home during my stay?
q7.answer: Home dialysis is not offered. We provide in-centre haemodialysis, in safe and supervised conditions, subject to validation by your referring nephrologist.

hero.title: Living through Ramadan on dialysis, with peace of mind
hero.subtitle: During Ramadan, our sessions are adapted in consultation with your nephrologist. Whether you fast or not, we organise your care to respect your choice and your health.

warning.title: Important — individualised medical decision
warning.body: Fasting during Ramadan while on dialysis is not recommended for all patients. Book an appointment with your nephrologist before Ramadan begins for a personalised assessment. No information on this page is a substitute for medical advice.

decision.title: Individualised medical decision
decision.text: Fasting during Ramadan is a decision that belongs to you — in consultation with your nephrologist. Some patients can fast, others cannot. We respect your choice and adapt your care accordingly.

premiere-seance.title: First session at 4 a.m.
premiere-seance.text: During Ramadan, we open a single set of slots with a first session at 4 a.m., before suhoor. No session after iftar, no night-time slot. The schedule is adjusted in consultation with your nephrologist.

vigilance.title: Heightened vigilance
vigilance.text: Our staff are briefed: closer monitoring of blood pressure, hydration and fluid balance. Any warning sign = breaking the fast as recommended by the doctor.

schedule.title: Sessions during Ramadan
schedule.intro: The exact schedule is communicated about 3 weeks before Ramadan begins.
schedule.point1: First session at 4 a.m. — before suhoor, for all patients (fasting or not).
schedule.point2: No session after iftar — and no night-time slot. The remaining slots of the day stay aligned with the usual pace.
schedule.ctaBody: We recommend seeing your nephrologist at least 2 weeks before Ramadan begins to assess your fitness to fast, adjust your medication and arrange your adapted sessions.
schedule.contact: Or contact us directly on <phone></phone> or via <whatsapp></whatsapp>.
schedule.whatsappMessage: Hello, I would like to adapt my sessions during Ramadan.

nutrition.title: Eating well during Ramadan
nutrition.intro: If your nephrologist allows you to fast, the way you organise your meals becomes decisive. The golden rule: three structured meals rather than continuous snacking, particular vigilance over hydration and salt, and measured choices when it comes to oriental sweets.

repas.title: Three structured meals
repas.text: Iftar, a light dinner in the evening, a substantial suhoor. Avoid continuous snacking: that is what protects your blood sugar and your interdialytic weight gain.

hydratation.title: Controlled hydration
hydratation.text: Your fluid allowance stays the same as outside Ramadan. Spread it between iftar and suhoor. No fizzy drinks, little very salty food.

dattes.title: Measured dates and sweets
dattes.text: Two dates at iftar, a balanced chorba, half a portion of pastry rather than a parade of them. Mind the potassium in dates and the phosphorus in sweets.

rompre.title: Break the fast without hesitation
rompre.text: Unusual fatigue, faintness, signs of hypo- or hyperglycaemia: break the fast immediately. Religious law permits it for medical reasons.

guide.title: The complete guide
guide.body: Iftar, suhoor, dates, chorba, pastries, hydration, blood sugar monitoring: find all the practical advice in our dedicated guide.

jeuner.q: Can I fast while on dialysis?
jeuner.a: This decision belongs to your nephrologist. For many dialysis patients, fasting remains possible provided there is close monitoring. Others cannot fast for medical reasons. Come for a consultation before Ramadan to take stock.

medicaments.q: What should I do about my medication?
medicaments.a: Your nephrologist adjusts when you take it (before iftar, between iftar and suhoor, or after suhoor depending on the medication). Never change a prescription on your own initiative. List all your treatments and let's discuss them together.

hydratation.q: How do I manage hydration and weight gain?
hydratation.a: This is the critical point. Between iftar and suhoor, strictly respect your permitted fluid volume (usually 500 ml + residual urine output). Avoid very salty food when breaking the fast. Interdialytic weight gain remains the limit not to be exceeded.

malaise.q: What should I do if I feel faint or my blood pressure drops?
malaise.a: Break the fast immediately — religious law permits breaking it for medical reasons. Call us as soon as possible. In an emergency, go to the nearest emergency department.

cafe.q: Is coffee served during sessions in Ramadan?
cafe.a: The single 4 a.m. session takes place before suhoor, therefore before the fast. Coffee can be served at suhoor at the end of the session, depending on your wishes and your clinical condition.

meta.title: Understanding kidney failure — an educational guide
meta.description: The role of the kidneys, the warning signs, the 5 stages, who should be screened, and how to slow progression. 10% of cases of kidney failure can be prevented, and 30% delayed by several years.
meta.ogDescription: An educational guide to chronic kidney disease: the role of the kidneys, signs, stages, screening, prevention.

hero.title: Understanding kidney failure
hero.subtitle: Chronic kidney disease is a silent illness. Caught early, it can be slowed, sometimes considerably. Here, in plain terms, is what you need to know.
hero.photoAlt: Anatomical cross-section of a human kidney showing the cortex around the outside, the medulla with the renal pyramids, the calyces and renal pelvis that collect urine, as well as the renal artery and vein entering at the hilum and the ureter.

intro.disclaimer: This page is for information purposes only. It does not replace the advice of your GP or your nephrologist, who remains the best person to assess your situation.
intro.title: Why this page
intro.lead: Chronic kidney disease sets in slowly, often quietly, and many people are unaware of it for years. The good news is that a simple blood test can pick it up early, and there are now real ways to protect your kidneys, slow the disease and carry on living a full life.

anatomie.title: What your kidneys do
anatomie.p1: You have two kidneys, located in the lower back, on either side of the spine. Each one weighs barely 160 grams — about the size of a large bean.
anatomie.p2: Think of them as two very fine filters. Day and night, all of your blood passes through them to be cleaned: they keep what is useful (proteins, blood cells) and let what the body no longer needs (excess water, salt, waste products such as urea and creatinine) pass out into the urine.
anatomie.p3: The kidneys do other things too: they help to regulate blood pressure, to keep bones strong and to make red blood cells. When they are working well, you don't feel them — and that is precisely why it is sometimes worth checking that they are in good order.

definition.title: What is chronic kidney disease?
definition.p1: The kidneys are made up of tiny microscopic filters called nephrons. When some of them are permanently damaged — by diabetes, high blood pressure or other causes — they do not regenerate. The kidney keeps working, but a little less well.
definition.p2: We talk about chronic kidney disease when this loss of function persists over time (more than three months). It is often painless and gradual, which is why it is frequently discovered during a routine check-up. The earlier it is detected, the more can be done.

nephron.imageAlt: Detailed diagram of a nephron: on the left the glomerulus with its afferent and efferent arterioles surrounded by Bowman's capsule; in the centre the tubules — proximal convoluted tubule, descending then ascending loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule; on the right the cortical and medullary collecting ducts.
nephron.caption: The nephron — the microscopic filtering unit. Each kidney contains around one million of them.
nephron.eyebrow: A closer look at the nephron
nephron.title: One million microscopic filters per kidney
nephron.p1: Each kidney contains around one million nephrons, the microscopic units that filter your blood continuously. In the diagram you can see the glomerulus — the filter itself, where blood passes through a fine membrane — and then the whole system of tubules that fine-tunes the composition of the urine by reclaiming what should be kept (water, salts, glucose) and letting the waste products flow on towards the collecting duct.
nephron.p2: This miniaturised mechanism is repeated one million times per kidney. As long as enough nephrons are working, the kidney compensates — which is why you can lose part of your kidney function without feeling anything. The catch: by the time the first symptoms appear, often fewer than 30% of nephrons are still working.
nephron.p3: Understanding the nephron means understanding why a simple blood test is so valuable: it measures creatinine, a waste product that healthy nephrons clear away. Its silent rise in the blood is the first signal — well before the body shows any sign.

signes.title: Which symptoms should raise the alarm?
signes.subtitle: Kidney failure advances quietly. But certain signals can put you on alert.
signes.evolutionTitle: Later on, these may be added
signes.calloutBody: Even at an advanced stage, before dialysis, you continue to pass urine normally. The absence of urine is therefore not a criterion. If several of these signs apply to you, talk to your doctor — it isn't necessarily the kidneys, but it is worth checking.

signesDebut: Unusual tiredness that appears even after light exertion
The need to get up several times during the night to pass urine
Rising blood pressure, or swelling (ankles, face)
signesEvolution: Digestive problems: aversion to meat, nausea, vomiting
Weight loss and more marked tiredness
Cramps or restless legs, especially at night

stades.title: Where do you stand on the map?
stades.subtitle: Doctors assess how well the kidneys are working using the glomerular filtration rate (GFR), calculated from a simple blood test.
stades.note: At stage 5, renal replacement therapy becomes necessary: dialysis or a transplant. This table is not a foregone conclusion: above all it is a map to find your bearings. Detecting the disease at stages 1, 2 or 3 means giving yourself years to slow its progression, sometimes very effectively. That is the whole point of screening.

depistage.whoSubtitle: A check at least once a year is a good habit if you are in one of these situations.
depistage.step1Body: Measurement of creatinine (a waste product that the kidneys normally clear away).
depistage.step2Body: Based on creatinine, your age and your weight (Cockcroft-Gault, MDRD, CKD-EPI formulas). The laboratory or the doctor takes care of it.
depistage.step3Body: To check for the presence of albumin, another useful marker.
depistage.note: If there is any doubt or an abnormal result, your doctor will refer you to a nephrologist for specialist advice.

facteursRisque: You have high blood pressure or diabetes
You have a history of cardiovascular disease (arteries, heart)
You have a family history of kidney disease or a genetic condition
You have had several upper urinary tract infections or recurrent kidney stones
You regularly take certain medicines that are hard on the kidneys (long-term anti-inflammatories, lithium, some chemotherapies…)

piliers.quote: “10% of cases of kidney failure can be prevented, and 30% can be delayed by several years — sometimes more than a decade.”
piliers.quoteCaption: Acting early changes the trajectory. The recommendations rest on four pillars.
piliers.label: Pillar {num}

pilier1.title: Adopt a few good habits
items: Stay active (30 minutes of walking a day)
Keep your vaccinations up to date
See your nephrologist at least once a year if you are at risk

pilier2.title: Keep your blood pressure well controlled
items: Measure your blood pressure at home when possible
Keep an eye on your weight
Take your treatment regularly, without skipping doses

pilier3.title: Protect your kidneys
items: Avoid medicines that tire the kidneys (NSAIDs without advice)
Moderate your intake of animal protein as advised by the nephrologist
Keep cholesterol, triglycerides and diabetes well balanced

pilier4.title: Understand your treatment and take part in it
items: Know your medicines and what they are for
Follow how your test results change over time
Speak openly during consultations, without holding back
An informed patient is a better-cared-for patient
